excerpts: Fine-Tuning with LoRA or QLoRA This is an example of using MLX to fine-tune an LLM with low rank adaptation (LoRA) for a target task. 1 The example also supports quantized LoRA (QLoRA). 2 The example works with Llama and Mistral style models available on Hugging Face.

By default, the adapter weights are saved in `adapters.npz` . You can specify
the output location with `--adapter-file` .
You can generate a fused model with the low-rank adapters included using the `fuse.py` script.
This will by default load the base model from `mlx_model/` , the adapters from `adapters.npz` , and save the fused model in the path `lora_fused_model/` .

To upload a fused model, supply the `--upload-name` and `--hf-path` arguments
to `fuse.py` .

executive_summary.description: A high-level summary of the current landscape for runtime LoRA adapter swapping on-device and in-browser for Gemma, highlighting the most viable options and their primary use cases.

technology_name.description: Name of the technology or framework.

primary_use_case.description: The primary environment where this technology is used (e.g., In-browser, On-mobile, Server-side).

hot_swapping_support.description: Describes the level of support for runtime adapter swapping without re-initialization (e.g., Full, Partial, None, Experimental).

supported_artifact_formats.description: The required file formats for the base model and LoRA adapters (e.g., ONNX, TFLite, GGUF, .safetensors).

adapter_management_api.description: Description of the API or methods used to load, set, and switch between adapters.

key_limitations.description: Summary of the main limitations, such as platform constraints, Gemma model support, or feature maturity.

technology_options_overview.description: An array providing a detailed analysis of each specified production option for LoRA adapter swapping. Each item will be expanded to cover hot-swapping support, artifact formats, APIs, and limitations for that specific technology.

transformers_js_analysis.description: In-depth analysis of Transformers.js for in-browser inference. Details include its use of ONNX with WebGPU, current support for PEFT/LoRA, and documented capabilities or limitations regarding runtime adapter swapping.

onnx_runtime_web_analysis.description: Analysis of ONNX Runtime, distinguishing between its general capabilities and the specific features of ONNX Runtime Web. Covers the status of LoRA support for in-browser execution and the APIs available in other environments (Python, C++).

mediapipe_llm_analysis.description: Detailed analysis of Google AI Edge's MediaPipe LLM framework for on-mobile deployment. Includes information on its support for Gemma, the LoRA model conversion process to TFLite, API for loading adapters, and limitations regarding dynamic runtime swapping.

llama_cpp_analysis.description: Analysis of llama.cpp, focusing on its server-based capabilities. Details its support for loading multiple LoRA adapters, the GGUF model format, and the API endpoints available for managing and potentially hot-swapping adapters at runtime.

mlx_analysis.description: Analysis of Apple's MLX framework. Covers its workflow for LoRA fine-tuning, the process of fusing adapters with a base model, and its current capabilities regarding applying different adapters at runtime without a full model reload.

executorch_analysis.description: Analysis of PyTorch's ExecuTorch framework. Explains its 'program-data separation' model for LoRA, which enables efficient memory usage by sharing a base model across multiple adapters, and its C++ API for running models.

hot_swapping_capability_comparison.description: A comparative summary of the 'hot-swapping' support across all evaluated frameworks. Clarifies which frameworks support true runtime swapping versus those that require model re-initialization.

artifact_format_and_conversion_summary.description: A summary of the different artifact formats (e.g., ONNX, .safetensors, TFLite, GGUF, NPZ, PTE) required for the base model and LoRA adapters by each framework, including any necessary conversion steps.

adapter_management_api_overview.description: An overview of the APIs provided by each framework for loading, initializing, setting, and switching between LoRA adapters during runtime.

framework_limitations_and_maturity.description: A discussion of the key limitations, platform constraints, and maturity of LoRA features within each framework, particularly concerning Gemma model support and the experimental nature of runtime swapping.

recommended_framework.description: The recommended framework for the Next.js browser demo (e.g., Transformers.js).

base_model_strategy.description: Strategy for loading and managing the base Gemma model in the browser, including caching and initialization.

adapter_handling_logic.description: Proposed logic for fetching, storing, and applying different LoRA adapters at runtime within the browser.

swapping_mechanism.description: The technical approach for swapping adapters, detailing whether it involves re-instantiating the model pipeline or a more seamless switch.

ui_ux_considerations.description: Considerations for the user interface to handle loading states and provide clear feedback to the user during adapter swaps.

code_structure_overview.description: A high-level overview of the proposed code structure within the Next.js application, including component and state management.
